data.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Large, phase-resolved haplotype panels—now emerging from efforts such as UK Biobank, TOPMed, All of Us, and the Mexican Biobank—enable fine-grained analyses of admixture, local ancestry, and imputation. The Positional Burrows–Wheeler Transform (PBWT) is a natural index for these data, supporting efficient phase-aware matching, but current methods typically enumerate all set-maximal exact matches (SMEMs), incurring unnecessary computation when only long matches are of interest. We introduce PBML, a space- and time-efficient algorithm that enumerates all SMEMs of length at least
                  <jats:italic>L</jats:italic>
                  . Our approach adapts Boyer–Moore style skipping with a forward–backward traversal of the PBWT, yielding an implementation that avoids auxiliary data structures while restricting search to informative long matches. We evaluate PBML under two querying strategies on two haplotype resources. On the 1000 Genomes Project (1KGP), PBML achieves up to 38.1% faster index construction than
                  <jats:italic>µ</jats:italic>
                  -PBWT, 61.7% faster single-threaded queries (with 22% higher peak memory), and a 305.7% multithreaded speedup (with only 9.4% higher peak memory), demonstrating strong scalability at population scale. We further show that, when targeting long SMEMs (
                  <jats:italic>L</jats:italic>
                  ≥ 10,000) on 1KGP, PBML reduces total runtime by 73.3% and peak memory usage by 20% relative to
                  <jats:italic>L</jats:italic>
                  = 1, while still reporting over 1.15 million long SMEMs genome-wide. Finally, we apply PBML to a biobank from the Tennessee Biorepository and Integrative Genomics (BIG) Initiative and show that, on chromosome 22, setting
                  <jats:italic>L</jats:italic>
                  between 500 and 1,000 preserves at least 95% single-query coverage (97–99.8% for batches of 5–20 queries) while reducing SMEM counts by 70–83.8% and cutting query time by 37.6%, with speedups up to 92.9% when
                  <jats:italic>L</jats:italic>
                  = 5,000 and only very long tracts are of interest. Together, these results establish PBML as an efficient and scalable tool to query PBWT-based haplotypes when biologically meaningful matches are the target.
                </jats:p>
data.title: Scalable PBWT Queries with Minimum-Length SMEM Constraints
